project.title: GlmS plays a key role in the virulence and biofilm formation ability of Staphylococcus aureus promoted by advanced glycation end products
project.projectDescription: Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) is well known for its biofilm formation ability and is responsible for serious, chronic refractory infections worldwide. We previously demonstrated that advanced glycation end products (AGEs), a hallmark of chronic hyperglycemia in diabetic tissues, enhanced biofilm formation by promoting eDNA release via sigB upregulation in S. aureus, contributing to the high morbidity and mortality of diabetic foot ulcer infection. However, the exact regulatory network has not been completely described. Here, we used a pull-down assay and LC‒MS/MS to identify the GlmS protein as a candidate regulator of sigB in S. aureus stimulated by AGEs. Dual-luciferase assays and electrophoretic mobility shift assays (EMSAs) revealed that GlmS directly upregulated the transcriptional activity of sigB. We constructed Newman ∆glmS for further validation. Quantitative RT‒PCR analysis revealed that AGEs promoted both glmS and sigB expression in the Newman strain but had no effect on Newman ∆glmS. Newman ∆glmS showed a significant attenuation in biofilm formation ability and virulence, accompanied by a decrease in sigB expression, even under AGE stimulation. All of the changes, including pigment deficiency, decreased hemolysis ability, downregulation of hla and hld expression, and less and sparser biofilms, indicated that sigB and biofilm formation ability no longer responded to AGEs in Newman ∆glmS. Our data extend the understanding of GlmS in the global regulatory network of S. aureus and demonstrate a new mechanism by which AGEs can upregulate GlmS, which subsequently directly regulates sigB and plays a significant role in mediating biofilm formation and virulence factor expression.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: “Set up a blank group and an experimental group respectively. The experimental group was promoted with 50 µg/mL AGEs in TSB broth and cultured for 24 hours. The blank group was promoted with the same concentration of BSA. Each group set four wells as M, Input, DPD and NC. M represents marker. Input represents the whole proteome of Newman. DPD represents SigB promoter probe pulldown proteins. NC represents negative probe pulldown proteins.”
project.dataProcessingProtocol: A pull-down assay was performed to analyze the interaction between biotin-labeled SigB promoter and the proteome of Newman as described previously. The biotin-labeled SigB promoter probe was synthesized by Axl-Bio (China) (F 5′-CAGCTATGACCATGATTACGAATTCTTGCAAACGACAAAATTGATAAGTGCAATTAAATAAATGTTAGTAAG-3′ and R 5′-GTAAAACGACGGCCAGTGCCAAGCTTTATTTCGCACCTGCTCTTTTTTTATATACTTAGTCATACTGAT-3′). Nucleoprotein extract (800 μl) was mixed with 4 μg of SigB promoter probe and 40 μl of streptavidin-agarose beads (Sigma, USA) and incubated at room temperature for two hours to pull down the DNA‒protein complexes. The complex was collected after incubation at 37 ℃ for two h and then boiled at 100 °C for further SDS‒PAGE analysis to separate the protein. The gel was stained with silver. Shotgun proteomics, one of LC‒MS/MS analysis was chosen to identify two groups of pulled-down proteins, and the protein peak maps were compared in the Uniprot database. The proteins that can bind to the SigB promoter in the AGEs group and the BSA group were identified and compared to find the differential proteins. Moreover, Gene Ontology (GO) functional analysis revealed that GlmS, a metabolite interconversion enzyme, has catalytic activity and participates in cellular processes and metabolic processes. Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) pathway analysis indicated that GlmS may be related to the N-acetylglucosamine metabolism pathway and glycolysis pathway. These characteristics had the greatest correlation with the promotion by AGEs in our experiment, so we selected GlmS protein as the candidate protein.
keywords: Staphylococcus aureus; advanced glycation end products; biofilm formation; glms-sigb regulatory axis

references.referenceLine: Ni L, Shen R, Luo H, Li X, Zhang X, Huang L, Deng Y, Liao X, Wu Y, Duan C, Xie X. GlmS plays a key role in the virulence factor expression and biofilm formation ability of Staphylococcus aureus promoted by advanced glycation end products. Virulence. 2024 15(1):2352476

identifiedPTMStrings.name: No PTMs are included in the dataset

Characteristics[Specimen]: About 800 µl of nucleoprotein extracted from NCTC 8325
Characteristics[SpikedCompound]: 4 µg of biotin-labelled SigB promoter probe
FactorValue[Treatment]: The blank groups were stimulated with the same concentration of bovine serum albumin (BSA) (EZ7890B203, BioFroxx, Germany).
Comment[EnrichmentMethod]: The pull-down assay utilized the natural affinity of streptomycin and biotin and was performed to analyse the interaction between DNA and proteins as described previously.
Comment[FractionationMethod]: The whole proteome and DNA pulled-down proteins of the NCTC 8325 strain were first separated by SDS-PAGE.

FactorValue[Treatment]: The experimental groups were stimulated with 50 µg/mL AGEs in TSB broth and cultured for 24 h.

Comment[FractionationMethod]: The whole proteome and DNA pulled-down proteins of the NCTC 8325 strain were first separated by SDS-PAGE.
